data.title: Broadcom: Like Buying Nvidia In May 2023
data.description:
Wednesday after the bell, Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) reported Q1 '26 earnings and got a big nod from a market that’s been punishing even double-beats this quarter. The stock was up a little over 7% in pre-market trading, really gaining momentum after CEO Hock Tan said the following midway through the earnings call:

We have line of sight to achieve AI revenue from chips, just chips, in excess of $100 billion in 2027. We have also secured the supply chain required to achieve this.

Broadcom is, simply put, the Nvidia (NVDA) of ASICs, and it has a longer runway ahead than behind in this AI cycle.

The market's reaction is cooling off in trading Thursday, with the stock now up only about 4% in reaction to the print.

For the quarter, Broadcom reported sales up 7% sequentially to $19.31B, comfortably ahead of the consensus of $19.14B. AI-related revenues were the star of the quarter, up 29% Q/Q to $8.4B on heightened demand for custom ASIC programs and data center switching. The company’s AI-related sales are shown below on a quarterly basis and have been growing rapidly over the past three quarters.

For Q2, management is guiding for this to persist, eyeing AI-related sales growth of 27% Q/Q and 143% Y/Y to $10.7B. For context, back in March 2024 (so about two years ago now), Broadcom guided AI-related demand to exceed $10B in FY25, up from $7.5B. Now, we’re talking about AI-related sales exceeding $100B in 2027 from its six ASIC customers.

Who Are The Lucky Six?

So, Broadcom has six ASIC customers under its belt, and each is ramping up AI accelerators. The catch with ASIC is that it is the lower-cost solution, meaning that as AI models become more mature, we’ll see more gravitation towards ASIC for low- and mid-level inference. Tan gave us a lot of info on the call yesterday that, if circulated correctly, tells of a massive upside story for Broadcom - one that could even spill into Marvell (MRVL).

So Broadcom has six customers, right? Alphabet/Google (GOOG), Meta (META), Anthropic (ANTHRO), Customers 4 and 5, and, lastly, OpenAI (OPENAI). Google is expected to grow in 2027 versus 2026, so let’s estimate this at at least 3 GW, while Anthropic is looking to ramp up 1 GW in 2026 and 3 GW in 2027. Meta is expected to be shipping its MTIA this year and to ramp up multi-GW deployment next year, so at least 2 GW. The interesting thing for us at Meta was that Tan came out very early in the call and addressed head-on rumors that Meta is facing internal chip design hurdles. Tan pretty clearly said:

Now, contrary to recent analyst reports, Meta's custom accelerator MTIA road map is alive and well. We're shipping now. And in fact, for the next-generation XPUs, we will scale to multiple gigawatts in '27 and beyond.

Next, we have customers 4 and 5, both of which are expected to double Y/Y for 2027. Lastly, we have OpenAI, Broadcom’s sixth customer, which is expected to ramp to over 1 GW in 2027. Add all these GW together for 2027, and we’re talking about 10 GW in 2027 altogether. Then, if we take that GW number and place it side by side with management’s forecast for $100B in AI revenue next year, we get an extremely tight gauge on Broadcom’s ASIC cost per GW. We owe analyst Stacy Rasgon a shoutout for the great question during Q&A that helped give us a ballpark for this number. Here’s the conversation between Rasgon and Tan:

Rasgon:

I don't know if this is for Hock or Kirsten, but I wanted to dig in a little more to this substantially more than $100 billion next year. I'm trying to just count up the gigawatts. I counted, I don't know, 8 or 9, you have 3 from Anthropic, 1 from OpenAI, so that's 4. You said Meta was multiple, so at least 2. That gets me to 6. Google, I figure, should be bigger than Meta, so like at least 3, that's 9 and then you got a few others. I had thought that your content per gigawatt was sort of, call it, in a $20 billion per gigawatt range. I guess what I'm asking is, is my math around the gigawatts you plan to ship in '27 correct?

Tan:

…you can look at it, gigawatts, which is the right way to look at it instead of dollars because that's how we sell our chips to. You have to realize we -- depending on our LLM customer, our 6 customers now -- sorry, not 5, 6, 6, the dollars per gigawatt chip dollars varies, sometimes quite dramatically. It does vary. But you're right, it's not far from the dollars you're talking about. And if you look at it by gigawatt in '27, we are seeing it getting close to 10 gigawatts.

So the number isn't locked in across the board, but we know it's much cheaper than Nvidia's at about $20B versus $50B. Now, it’s important to remember that Nvidia’s AI GPUs and Broadcom’s ASICs each have a different function when it comes to training versus inference. So, this isn’t an overnight Broadcom takeover story, but it does signal that where AI is headed, Broadcom and ASIC will play a bigger role.

Infrastructure Software Isn't Dead Weight

The good news is that the other side of the business, Infrastructure Software, is not doing too badly, either. For the quarter, revenue was up slightly Y/Y, with operating margin holding steady at 78% and gross margin at 93%, an extremely high-margin business with the benefit of VMware integration, which specifically grew 13% Y/Y. The outlook is for growth to accelerate next year by 9% Y/Y to $7.2B.

We also got some commentary from Tan on the whole panic regarding AI cannibalizing software and the likes of VMware. He instead noted that VMware Cloud Foundation is needed for a layer between AI software and the physical hardware world. In Tan’s words, "generative and agentic AI will create the need for more VMware, not less." Now, we make a habit of taking management’s words with a grain of salt, but we see some basis here because the software selloff we saw on the back of AI panic is, in our opinion, overdone.

The Stock Is Cheap

The stock price U-turned for the worse after Broadcom’s last print in December, shedding nearly 20% in the couple of days that followed. Investors got spooked by AI bubble panic and the sustainability of the hyper-CapEx spend. Management also didn’t help, signaling pressure on gross margins from AI rack system sales. We stuck to our bullish conviction on Broadcom, particularly as the stock pulled back to its EMA50 support on the weekly chart, which sits at about $301 per share.

The stock has been left in the dust for some time since being an in-line performer with the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) in Q3 '25, up 20% versus the SOX, up 15%, and in Q4 '25, up 6% versus the SOX, up 11%. The stock has been underperforming this quarter, down 9% versus the SOX, up 14%. This print just knocked Broadcom back into investors’ radar; consistent with our expectations, the fundamentals outlive the panic.

Now, on Broadcom’s valuation: it’s no secret that Broadcom is expensive, and that is part of why the stock turned red on the last earnings report. That said, multiples have compressed dramatically since then, while growth has expanded. Broadcom trades at a forward P/E of 27.18 versus 61.20x when we last covered the stock post-Q4 print in December and EV/Sales of 15.36x compared to 31.59x last print.

Also worth noting is that while Broadcom does still sit above the peer group average, the gap between its current metrics and its historical averages is much smaller, signaling that Broadcom has been an above-sector performer for a while, i.e., this premium isn't necessarily new or panic-worthy. It also points to the fact that the VMware acquisition and subsequent integration have substantially re-scaled the business, and that’s part of what we’re seeing baked into multiples.

What To Do With The Stock?

Broadcom continues to be among our favorite picks in the semi front. The market opened on Thursday, and the stock pulled back from the +7% in pre-market to about +3% now. We think this is a market that’s confused, but the good news is that numbers don’t lie. The company is on a golden track as the AI market segmentizes, and AI GPUs are forced to share some of the market with ASICs.

As shown in the chart above, both Nvidia and Broadcom have been underperforming the S&P 500 benchmark, even more drastically than the SOX. We think investors shouldn't wait until the market wakes up to the potential and instead start building a stake at current levels in time for 2H26 and 2027 upside.

data.title: PepGen: Stock Slides On FDA Study Hold - I'm Firmly On The Sidelines
data.description:
Investment Overview

The stock of PepGen Inc. (PEPG) is down >20% in early trading today, after the company released its Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings, and shared the news that the FDA had placed a partial clinical hold on its FREEDOM2-DM1 Phase 2 multiple ascending dose ("MAD"), randomized, placebo-controlled clinical trial of candidate PGN-EDODM1 in patients with myotonic dystrophy type 1 ("DM1").

In its 2025 annual report / 10-K filing - also released today, PepGen discusses its business as follows:

PepGen Inc. is a clinical-stage biotechnology company advancing the next generation of oligonucleotide therapeutics with the goal of transforming the treatment of severe neuromuscular and neurologic diseases.

Our proprietary Enhanced Delivery Oligonucleotide, or EDO, platform is founded on over a decade of research and development and leverages cell-penetrating peptides, or CPPs, to improve the uptake and activity of conjugated oligonucleotide therapeutics.

Its lead and only candidate is PGN-EDODM1, discussed as follows in PepGen's earnings release:

PGN-EDODM1 utilizes the Company's proprietary EDO technology to deliver a therapeutic oligonucleotide that is designed to restore the normal splicing function of MBNL1, a key RNA splicing protein. PGN-EDODM1 addresses the deleterious effects of cytosine-uracil-guanine (CUG) repeat expansion in the dystrophia myotonica protein kinase (DMPK) transcripts which sequester MBNL1, by binding to the pathogenic CUG trinucleotide repeat expansion present in the DMPK transcripts, and disrupting the binding between the CUG repeat expansion and MBNL1.

Clearly, this is advanced science that is beyond the understanding of most investors, myself included. However, in a Phase 1 study, the drug demonstrated "dose dependent, best-in-class splicing correction" following a single dose, and "robust, greater than dose proportional increase in muscle tissue concentration," following a single dose.

As per PepGen's 10-K filing:

DM1 is a monogenic, autosomal dominant, progressive disorder that primarily affects skeletal, cardiac and smooth muscles, with central nervous system, or CNS, symptoms also being evident. Globally, the prevalence of DM1 is estimated to be 1 in 8,000 people, with approximately 40,000 patients in the U.S., 75,000 patients in Europe and 15,000 patients in Japan.

DM1 patients can suffer from various manifestations of disease including myotonia, or a temporary rigidity due to the inability to relax muscles, muscle weakness, cardiac abnormalities, respiratory problems, fatigue, gastrointestinal complications, early cataracts, and cognitive and behavioral impairments. For patients with more severe forms of DM1, life expectancy is reduced due to increased mortality rates resulting from pulmonary and cardiac complications.

Analysis: Understanding The FDA's Decision To Place A Partial Clinical Hold On FREEDOM Study

According to a PepGen release regarding the FDA's clinical hold:

The partial clinical hold questions raised by FDA relate to previously submitted preclinical pharmacology and toxicology studies. The partial clinical hold did not cite any questions regarding blinded clinical data from the Phase 1 FREEDOM study previously submitted to the FDA in order to initiate the FREEDOM2 study in the U.S. As part of its ongoing dialogue with the FDA, the Company is submitting additional analyses, including the recently unblinded FREEDOM data, and is committed to working with the FDA to address their questions as quickly as possible.

In its earnings release, PepGen pledged to share data from the 5mg/kg dose cohort of its FREEDOM-2 study, which includes 8 patients - sometime this quarter. A study overview is provided below from a March corporate presentation.

PepGen also notes that:

After reviewing safety data from the 5 mg/kg cohort, the Data Safety Monitoring Board (DSMB) recommended escalating to the 10 mg/kg dose. PepGen has since dosed 50% of patients (4/8) in the 10 mg/kg cohort with up to two doses. The Company remains on track to report results from the 10 mg/kg cohort in the second half of 2026.

No U.S. patients have been enrolled in the FREEDOM2 study to date, however the study is open and enrolling patients - including in the 10mg/kg cohort - in the United Kingdom and Canada, and has recently been cleared to open in New Zealand, Australia and South Korea.

PepGen stated in its press release:

The partial clinical hold did not cite any questions regarding blinded clinical data from the Phase 1 FREEDOM study previously submitted to the FDA in order to initiate the FREEDOM2 study in the U.S. As part of its ongoing dialogue with the FDA, the Company is submitting additional analyses, including the recently unblinded FREEDOM data, and is committed to working with the FDA to address their questions as quickly as possible.

PepGen is also running the FREEDOM-OLE, which is an open label extension ("OLE") study open to participants who meet its eligibility criteria and have completed either the FREEDOM or FREEDOM2 studies. To date, only patients in Canada have been enrolled in this study, however PepGen it intends to open the study to study participants in all geographies where FREEDOM2 is open.

Analysis: Hold News A Setback, Not Necessarily A Disaster

As clinical holds go, today's news does not seem too disastrous for PepGen (some analysts have apparently it down to staff shortages at the FDA). It has not been triggered by a patient death or a serious adverse event, seemingly, and the study continues in multiple overseas locations.

An analysis of safety data from the first FREEDOM study is shown below:

As we can see, there were no adverse events leading to study discontinuation, or to death, and only a single related serious adverse event - severe abdominal pain.

It may therefore be the case that the hold can be resolved speedily - data provided by the National Institutes for Health ("NIH") based on analysis of 33 clinical holds that were publicly announced from January 2020 to December 2022, suggests 80% were lifted, and after an average period of 6.2 months.

Of course, it could be the case that the FDA has spotted some inconsistencies in the preclinical data supplied to the by PepGen, which could be problematic for the company - as per its 10-K:

An IND (investigational New Drug application) is a request for authorization from the FDA to administer an investigational product to humans and must become effective before human clinical trials may begin. Some preclinical testing, such as animal tests of reproductive adverse events and carcinogenicity, may continue even after the IND is submitted.

An IND automatically becomes effective 30 days after receipt by the FDA, unless before that time the FDA raises concerns or questions related to one or more proposed clinical trials and places the clinical trial on a clinical hold. In such a case, the IND sponsor and the FDA must resolve any outstanding concerns before the clinical trial can begin. As a result, submission of an IND may not result in the FDA allowing clinical trials to initiate.

Clearly, PepGen will encounter real problems if the FDA has acted in response to data it feels has been misrepresented, of it feels that the preclinical data has been undermined in any way, but as discussed above, there is no way of truly knowing until further updates are shared, and statistically speaking, it seems likely the FREEDOM2 study will - at some stage - be permitted to enroll in the U.S.

Analysis: Early Days For PepGen, But Path Ahead Looks Daunting

PepGen's approach to treating DM1 may be novel, but splicing therapies are not so rare. For example, Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT) therapies Eteplirsen (Exondys 51), Golodirsen (Vyondys 53), Viltolarsen (Viltepso) and Casimersen (Amondys 45) are approved for Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy ("DMD") patients amenable to EXON 51,53,53 and 45 skipping (exon's are faulty, disease causing genes) respectively.

There is a catch, however. These therapies were granted "accelerated approval" by the FDA, which means they were approved based on a surrogate endpoint, i.e., a biomarker that is thought to predict clinical benefit but is not itself a measure of clinical benefit - splicing would likely fall in that category.

Last November, Sarepta reported that Vyondys 53 and Amondys 45 did not show a statistically significant difference to placebo based on an efficacy endpoint - patient mobility (DMD is a more severe form of DM1), arguably undermining the mechanism of action ("MoA") of the drug, and the gene splicing thesis more broadly.

In fact, PepGen previously tested a candidate - PGN-EDO51 - in DMD, but the candidate came up short, and management opted to end all of its work in that indication, despite positive Phase 1 healthy volunteer data. The company warns in its 10-K:

Accordingly, the results observed in our preclinical and Phase 1 clinical studies of PGN-EDODM1 may not be repeated in our ongoing Phase 2 clinical study. Moreover, regulatory authorities may disagree with the interpretation of data from our trials.

PepGen is far from the only company developing drug candidates to treat DM1 - as per its 10-K filing:

Pipeline candidates currently in development to treat DM1 include several approaches that target DMPK RNA. These include AOC 1001, an antibody linked siRNA in Phase 3 clinical development by Avidity Biosciences, Inc., (RNA) or Avidity, which has agreed to be acquired by Novartis, subject to various closing conditions;

DYNE-101, an antibody conjugated antisense oligonucleotide in Phase 2 clinical development by Dyne (DYN), with an ongoing registrational expansion cohort reportedly intended to support a potential submission for accelerated approval in the U.S. and with plans by Dyne to initiate a confirmatory Phase 3 clinical trial of DYNE-101 in Q1 2026;

VX-670, a peptide conjugated PMO in Phase 2 originally developed by Entrada Therapeutics, Inc., or Entrada (TRDA), and Vertex (VRTX); and ARO-DM1, a conjugated siRNA in Phase 1/2 clinical development by Sarepta, originally developed by Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals, Inc., or Arrowhead (ARWR).

There are additional approaches under development such as ATX-01, a microRNA that modulates expression of MBNL1 by Arthex Biotech S.L., that is in Phase 1/2a clinical development. Another small molecule, tideglusib, which is a GSK3-ß inhibitor is in clinical development by AMO for the congenital phenotype of DM1.

In short, them, PepGen is some way behind the development curve, and although its claim that the 53.7% mean splicing correction at the 15mg dose observed in its MAD study of PGN-EDODM1 is impressive, this was observed in a patient population of just eight, and we cannot even say for certain that this will translate to a meaningful efficacy benefit.

Concluding Thoughts: Is There A Contrarian Opportunity In Play After Today's PepGen News?

PepGen has encountered clinical holds in the past, and not just in the U.S. - the company voluntarily paused its CONNECT2-EDO51 study in the United Kingdom, which was already subject to a clinical hold in the U.S., pending results of a study in Canada.

Both DM1 and DMD have proven to be exceptionally hard to treat effectively - Sarepta was forced to take its Elevidys gene therapy off the market briefly last year as a result of patient deaths - and in my view, it is hard to make the case at this time that there are compelling reasons to believe PepGen has a candidate with a real chance of approval in PGN-EDODM1, despite some early data suggesting "best-in-class" splicing.

With that said, PepGen stock is certainly cheap - trading at $5.60 at the time of writing, which conveys a market cap of ~$382m. Any biotech with a <$500m market cap should be considered a high risk investment opportunity, however, and financially speaking, the company reported $163.7m of cash, equivalents, and short-term investments as of the end of 2025, which management says is sufficient to fund operations into the second half of 2027. Operating loss in 2025 was $(93.6m), and $(97.7m) in 2024, with R&D expenses of $71m and $76m respectively.

In order to continue its work beyond 2027 given this cash burn, then, PepGen would likely need to raise more funding, possibly via a dilutive (for existing investors) share offering, and management will doubtless be hoping for a compelling data readout that would send its share price soaring, allowing it to complete a fundraising at a higher share price.

PepGen stock is in fact up >320% on a six-month basis - the best-in-class splicing data sending stock soaring in September last year. However, personally, rather than be excited by the surge, I'd be fearful that the next data set is less compelling (as was the case in DMD), and shares fall back to just over $1 per share.

I hope I am wrong and PepGen can succeed in DM1 where it could not in DMD, and share some positive efficacy data, and a clean safety profile, in a mid-stage DM1 study, and I would absolutely not rule that out - it seems the splicing thesis is not going anywhere - but I find myself firmly on the sidelines with regard to the investment opportunity here.

data.title: 5 Best Dividend Stocks To Combine Growth, Income, And Defense In 2026
data.description:
Dividend Stocks That Can Thrive in a Challenging 2026 Macro Backdrop

Investors entered 2026 expecting inflation to moderate, interest rates to gradually fall, and geopolitical tensions to remain modestly high but contained. However, reality has presented a more challenging set of circumstances. A resilient labor market and rising gas prices tied to rising tensions in the Middle East have helped keep inflation elevated. As a result, the Federal Reserve’s "higher for longer" rate path expectation has returned, creating a challenging macro backdrop for stocks.

First Rate Cut Pushed to July 2026 or Later

An uncertain tariff policy and fluctuating economic data have also contributed to volatility, making diversification and mindful sector rotation increasingly important for portfolios.

In this backdrop, the most attractive opportunities may include companies that combine reliable dividend income, strong profitability metrics, and forward growth potential. Certain sectors, especially those tied to physical assets, defense spending, real estate income, consumer staples, and select technology, can offer these opportunities. With that in mind, the following five stocks stand out as attractive dividend-paying stocks that can potentially thrive even as inflation remains sticky and markets remain volatile

How I Chose the Best Dividend Stocks for Growth, Income, and Defense

To find the best dividend stocks for growth and income, as well as defensive qualities, I used the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener and started with the preset Top Dividend Stocks filter and customized it to include the sector screen so I could sort for materials, consumer staples, real estate, aerospace and defense, and technology. This combination of sectors provides the diversification aspect of the mix.

1. Newmont Corporation (NEM)

  • Market Capitalization: $129.67B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/05/2026): 23 out of 279

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/05/2026): 13 out of 49

  • Sector: Materials

  • Industry: Gold

  • Yield: 0.87

Gold equities have long played a role in portfolios during periods of geopolitical uncertainty and inflation risk, and Newmont is one of the sector’s most prominent dividend-paying companies. As a world-leading gold miner, the company offers direct leverage to gold prices while maintaining the scale and diverse operations that smaller miners often lack. With inflation proving stubborn and geopolitical tensions fueling safe-haven demand, gold has the potential to remain well supported in the current macro environment, especially after today’s U.S. dollar strength fades. From a Quant perspective, Newmont’s profitability is a key strength, as it provides the cash flow cushion needed to withstand cyclical shifts while still returning capital to shareholders.

NEM’s EBITDA margin of more than 61% is nearly 260% higher than the sector median, while its Cash From Operations metric of 10.33B is an incredible 2,189% higher than sector peers. When a miner can operate with strong margins like this, it can remain profitable in challenging macro environments, which may be the case for 2026. This flexibility helps ensure the company can maintain dividends even during periods when gold prices fluctuate.

While Newmont’s dividend yield of 0.87% isn’t high, it’s stable and higher than that of the S&P 500 Index, which is a quality that belongs in a diverse mix of income-generating stocks. The company’s 15% Cash Dividend Payout Ratio, which is comfortably below sector peers, supports its 'A-' dividend safety grade. This healthy Quant score is particularly important for a cyclical business like gold mining, and it provides a clearer picture of whether a company can comfortably fund its dividend. While gold miners offer protection against macro shocks, another area benefiting directly from geopolitical tensions is the defense industry, which I highlighted in a recent article, where rising global military spending continues to support long-term growth.

2. Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT)

  • Market Capitalization: $152.88B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/05/2026): 30 out of 611

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/05/2026): 6 out of 65

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Aerospace and Defense

  • Yield: 2.08%

Few industries are as closely tied to geopolitical environments as defense, and Lockheed Martin remains one of the largest and most important contractors supplying advanced military systems to the United States and its allies. From missile defense platforms to advanced fighter aircraft, the company is at the center of global defense. With geopolitical tensions rising in the Middle East and remaining elevated in Eastern Europe, defense spending among NATO members and other allied nations is likely to stay elevated for the long term. Like many Quant Strong Buy stocks with attractive dividend metrics, Lockheed’s 'A+' profitability provides solid support.

Lockheed’s standout Return on Common Equity of nearly 77% is more than 6x the sector median, and this highlights the company’s ability to generate strong profits and maintain competitive advantages in the defense sector. Meanwhile, its sector-crushing 8.56B Cash From Operations metric demonstrates that those profits translate into consistent, real cash generation from long-term government contracts. This combination signals a highly profitable and financially resilient business model, which also provides the foundation for Lockheed’s strong and steadily growing dividend.

LMT has 23 consecutive years of dividend growth, which compares to three years for the sector median. Lockheed’s long-term government contracts provide unusually stable revenue visibility, a key advantage during uncertain economic environments like 2026. While defense spending can provide durable revenue streams, investors seeking income in volatile markets may also find stability in real estate businesses that generate recurring rental income through long-term lease agreements.

3. Alpine Income Property Trust, Inc. (PINE)

  • Market Capitalization: $318.58M

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/05/2026): 5 out of 170

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 02/26/2026): 1 out of 12

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Diversified REITs

  • Yield: 6.14%

REITs like Alpine Income Property Trust can play a valuable role in income-oriented portfolios, particularly those structured around long-term leases with high-quality tenants. I covered this REIT in a recent top dividend stocks article, explaining how it can work well as a hedge against geopolitical uncertainty and inflation. Here I’ll provide another angle, beginning with the company’s focus on the net-lease segment of the market, where tenants typically sign multi-year contracts that provide landlords with predictable cash flows. This stability has yielded a Quant Strong Buy rating for PINE, led by its growth, momentum, and revisions metrics.

PINE’s sector-leading AFFO growth metrics signal that the REIT is successfully expanding the cash flow available to support and grow its dividend, often through disciplined acquisitions and rent escalations. At the same time, strong Operating Cash Flow Growth indicates that its properties and tenant relationships are generating increasing rental income. These factors support both dividend stability and long-term growth.

While real estate income streams offer stability, investors seeking defensive dividends may also look to consumer staples stocks with strong profitability metrics.

4. Philip Morris International Inc. (PM)

  • Market Capitalization: $278.71B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/05/2026): 15 out of 176

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/05/2026): 2 out of 10

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Tobacco

  • Yield: 3.28%

Consumer staples companies often serve as core holdings in income portfolios, especially in uncertain times, because demand for their products tends to remain steady regardless of broader economic conditions. Philip Morris International is a good example, with its global portfolio of tobacco and nicotine products generating consistent cash flows even during periods of economic volatility. The company has also been investing heavily in its transition toward smoke-free products, including heated tobacco and other lower-risk alternatives. These investments provide potential forward growth coupled with its strong profitability, which is the machine that supports outstanding dividends.

The company’s strong pricing power has historically allowed it to offset inflationary pressures, helping maintain margins while continuing to pay dividends to shareholders. PM’s Gross Profit Margin of 67% is more than 35% above the sector median, making it a standout amid its consumer staples peers. The company’s Cash From Operations of 12.23B is more than 14x the sector median, helping to support its healthy 3.28% yield and impressive dividend scorecard.

The combination of strong profitability and sector-crushing cash flow generation has enabled Philip Morris to maintain one of the more attractive dividend yields among large global consumer staples stocks. Beyond defensive sectors, investors may also find dividend opportunities in select technology firms with strong free cash flows.

5. Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL)

  • Market Capitalization: $97.48B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/05/2026): 15 out of 526

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/05/2026): 2 out of 26

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Technology Hardware, Storage and Peripherals

  • Yield: 1.71%

While technology companies are not typically associated with income investing, Dell Technologies has emerged as an example of how strong free cash flow can support both growth and dividends. The company sits at the center of enterprise computing infrastructure, supplying servers, storage systems, and networking hardware used by corporations and cloud providers around the world. As the AI buildout accelerates, demand for Dell's equipment has increased, creating a significant growth driver for the company's expansion in this segment. As for its Quant metrics that make a solid growth and income combination, we’ll start with its 'A+' Profitability Factor Grade.

While Dell’s Gross Profit Margin scores below the sector median, a much stronger predictor for income generation is Cash From Operations. This metric is 11.19B for Dell, which is an incredible 7,469% above the sector median. Dell’s strong operating cash flow has allowed the company to steadily return capital to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases while continuing to invest in its forward growth. This profitability strength has enabled the company to stand above its technology sector peers for its dividend yield, earning it a solid 'B+' for this Quant Factor.

Together, these characteristics highlight how select technology names can offer investors exposure to growth trends while still delivering income. With this final piece in place, the broader mix of five stocks illustrates how dividend-paying companies across multiple sectors can help investors build a portfolio in uncertain markets.

Conclusion: Best Dividend Stocks for 2026

Periods of persistent inflation, higher interest rates, and geopolitical tension can create a challenging environment for investors seeking stability and defensive qualities without giving up growth. Companies that can offer these qualities, including large, established gold miners, consumer staples stocks, income-generating real estate companies, and select tech stocks. By combining reliable dividends backed by outstanding profitability, these five companies illustrate how investors can build portfolios that can withstand volatility while participating in potential upside in 2026.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top "strong buy" Quant stocks. Seeking Alpha’s quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

If you’re looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It’s a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps. Happy investing!

data.title: This One Data Readout Could Make Or Break Revolution Medicines
data.description:
Thesis

I last covered Revolution Medicines (RVMD) back in late January. If you remember back then, the stock had just plummeted due to news that Merck (MRK) had just walked away from talks about an acquisition. At the time, it brought the stock price down from the $120 range to the $100 range. I am choosing to cover Revolution again, specifically now because of two things. Firstly, a couple of weeks ago, we saw them report FY25/4Q25 earnings, but I also wanted to wait until after we heard from management at the TD Cowen Annual Conference. As you know, the upcoming RASolute 302 readout is going to be a major catalyst for this stock, so I think it's worth taking a look at what management told us about the upcoming readout. We can expect the data sometime in 1H26. As I will explain, the main thing to be focused on here is the overall survival rate we see in the trial. As for the current stock price, despite the sell-off at the end of January, shares still look heavily overvalued. That's because a large portion of a positive outcome for Revolution this year has been priced into the stock. It makes shares quite high risk, and for that reason, I have chosen to go with a hold rating again on the stock. However, I am going to give my thoughts on what we heard from management on the call and assess the potential upside of a positive trial outcome. In my previous coverage, I gave a detailed look at the potential TAM for daraxonrasib as well as how the treatment works.

4Q25/FY25 Financial Recap

A couple of weeks ago, Revolution reported FY25/4Q25 results. I think it's safe to say we saw some higher operating costs that resulted in a widening net loss for FY25. But this was somewhat expected since they’re trying to transition into a late-stage, Phase 3 development biotech, a status which naturally comes with higher research costs. For FY25, the R&D expense jumped to about $987.3 million, up from the $592.2 million we saw back in 2024. It’s a hefty 67% increase, which we can put down mainly to the expanded global Phase 3 trials for both daraxonrasib and zoldonrasib. There have also been some higher manufacturing costs, and headcount growth to help support those clinical operations.

As for G&A, the spend here nearly doubled to $195.0 million, so they’re clearly preparing for commercial readiness and a push in terms of corporate infrastructure. So overall, this added up to a net loss of $1.13 billion for FY25 versus the $600.1 million loss in 2024. This tells me just how capital-intensive their pipeline is getting now with the pushing of multiple assets toward potential registration, something that's not cheap by any means. But importantly, we can classify this loss as largely operational here rather than financial-engineering-driven. Interest income is still pretty high from their large cash position, and there was just minimal offset from some non-operating items.

So even with these, I feel the balance sheet is still quite strong; it's a big strength of the company in 2026. Revolution managed to end the year with about $2.0 billion in cash, equivalents, and marketable securities. This was also helped out by that $250 million royalty monetization tranche and an extra $1.75 billion in committed future capital from Royalty Pharma. So even with the expected 2026 GAAP operating expenses falling in the range of $1.6 to $1.7 billion, they still seem to be pretty well-funded going into this most critical value-inflexion period. That period would include the likes of the pivotal RASolute 302 Phase 3 readout in pancreatic cancer, which I see as one of their biggest near-term catalysts. Revolution's flexibility this year is actually a bit unusual if you compare it to similar clinical-stage biotechs, in the sense that they sustain multiple parallel registrational trials while still pursuing combination strategies. Even if we were to see potential delays, I would expect Revolution to be able to absorb them without too much immediate dilution risk.

RASolute 302 Readout

So another big reason I’ve chosen to cover Revolution again now is the RASolute 302 readout. Specifically, I wanted to wait until after we heard from management at the conference this week to assess the situation. Now, on the call, I felt management essentially made it unmistakably clear that the RASolute 302 readout is the defining event for the company. I have to agree with them; it’s a vital catalyst for the stock. You see, it’s the first randomized test of whether direct RAS inhibition can actually extend survival in pancreatic cancer. Now, the company designed this trial to answer a very specific but very ambitious question: Can daraxonrasib actually beat chemotherapy in previously treated pancreatic cancer? And this has to be true not just based on tumor shrinkage but on meaningful clinical benefit. Below is the current timeline for Daraxonrasib. Since there are quite a few ongoing trials, it can be hard to keep up.

The current durability data would suggest that daraxonrasib is actually doing more than producing short-lived tumor shrinkage. So far, in both patients and preclinical models, responses appear to be quite sustained over time, which would indicate prolonged suppression of RAS signaling rather than just rapid resistance.

So the study itself is structured with a core cohort of patients carrying G12X mutations, which makes up about 85% of pancreatic cancer. Just to touch again briefly on this mutation, the G12X refers to a specific genetic alteration in the KRAS gene. This is the main oncogenic driver in pancreatic cancer that we see. This gene position is pretty critical because it sits in that KRAS switch region that can control whether the protein is turned on or off. So when that mutation hits at codon 12, KRAS essentially becomes locked in an active, growth-signaling state. It then continuously tells the cell to divide and survive, which is what we obviously don't want.

The study undergoes the primary statistical analysis first, and then they follow it with a broader analysis that includes other mutations such as G13, Q61, and potentially a RAS-wildtype. This is somewhat of a layered design, so that tells me what management is thinking here, in that they feel RAS biology is central in pancreatic cancer, but also they acknowledge regulatory rigor. Let me explain this. By putting patients with that G12X mutation into a core cohort that gets analyzed first, I believe it suggests that Revolution thinks that pancreatic cancer is fundamentally a RAS-driven disease and that this is the specific population where daraxonrasib has the highest likelihood of showing benefit. I think this is a smart move. They have far more clinical/preclinical data in this group, and therefore, there should be more confidence here that inhibiting RAS will actually translate into improved outcomes. But on the regulatory side of things, they cannot ignore the rest of pancreatic cancer. So they structured the trial with a broader, secondary analysis that includes those other mutations I mentioned. I think of it as a hierarchy of analyzes that allows them to first test their strongest biological case while still preserving that ability to evaluate efficacy in the full pancreatic cancer population.

Management's View Seems Bullish

Now, on the conference call, the CEO, Mark Goldsmith, gave us three possible outcomes for the trial, and I felt it was quite a blunt answer. He said that they could pass PFS, pass PFS statistical significance, and not yet be significant for OS, or that they could pass statistical significance for both PFS and OS. It’s safe to say that what matters most is the last outcome; so, daraxonrasib significantly delays disease progression and also extends overall survival compared with chemotherapy. And that's because the company is explicitly trying to prove here that suppressing RAS specifically changes how long patients live, not just how long scans look stable. Below is from the CEO.

“It's possible that there could be a range of potential scenarios just being objective and not leaning into any particular scenario, we could not pass PFS, and therefore, not pass OS. We could pass PFS statistical significance and not yet be significant for OS or we could pass statistical significance for both PFS and OS. Those are just 3 objective potential outcomes and they lead to different consequences.”

Now, Goldsmith also stressed on the call that response rates are not actually the right metric in pancreatic cancer. His reasoning is that patients aren’t asking if they’re going to have a 32% or 37% response, but rather they’re asking how long they’re going to live. It means tumor shrinkage/response rate doesn’t fully capture the clinical benefit in pancreatic cancer. As you know, in some other cancers, shrinking tumors can actually reliably predict longer survival. But in pancreatic cancer, a patient’s durability of benefit and overall survival matter a lot more. That is why overall survival is the moat for Revolution here, in that if daraxonrasib shows a survival benefit, it gives them a new benchmark that competitors have to beat. This is the key to anchoring pricing and adoption globally; this basically makes the bull case for me. Also, the overall survival creates a pretty durable competitive advantage simply due to it being so much harder to dislodge than PFS or response rate. This is particularly true in a disease as lethal as pancreatic cancer.

Revolution is Operationally Prepared

Another big talking point on the call was management detailing that they’re very much operationally prepared to move straight into an NDA if the data is positive. We now know that they have aligned manufacturing, clinical documentation systems, and FDA engagement to support as fast a submission as they really can. Management also wants the FDA review to be in a staged fashion, more consistent with an accelerated, rolling-style process here rather than a single last-minute package. If we think about it commercially, this company has spent about two years building launch infrastructure in parallel with the trial. That includes medical affairs coverage and an unbranded education campaign like Expect RAS to help to prime physicians for a RAS-targeted therapy in pancreatic cancer.

So leadership roles are already being filled, such that it puts marketing and access teams in place for Revolution. Even field sales hiring has begun, so it leaves the only true gating factor being the clinical data. Which goes to show just how important this readout is. We also have to think here that being ready for a positive overall survival result isn't a bad thing. It could pretty quickly translate into regulatory action and help reduce that typical lag between breakthrough data and patient access.

Valuation

Revolution’s current valuation looks rather extreme on conventional metrics. But keep in mind that they’re still a pre-revenue, late-stage oncology company. They have no meaningful earnings and minimal product revenue. They hold an enterprise value of about $18.5 billion and a price-to-book ratio sitting at 12x. The company also trades at a massive EV-to-sales ratio simply because current sales are negligible. So investors here are essentially valuing Revolution on future cash flow, but also a lot on the probability-weighted future cash flows of daraxonrasib. I think the balance sheet helps a lot here with this risk-taking. Over $2 billion in cash gives them a runway to execute multiple Phase 3 trials and also fund the commercialization without near-term dilution, which justifies some premium relative to their smaller biotech peers.

Looking Ahead

What really makes the stock look expensive here is what is being priced in. The market is basically pricing in that the upcoming RASolute-302 readout will be positive and that daraxonrasib will win regulatory approval in second-line pancreatic cancer. The market has also started to price in the fact that this will expand into earlier lines and combination regimens across pancreatic and lung cancer, in my opinion. If you look at the one-year chart, it’s run up about 166%. So this valuation reflects expectations of a first-in-class RAS franchise with multi-billion-dollar peak sales potential. The market isn’t just pricing in a single drug launch here. Investors are also paying up a bit for the strategic moat management is trying to build around overall survival data. I can’t disagree with them here. If we do see daraxonrasib showing a benefit on survival, I believe it would pretty much anchor a new standard of care in the space.

data.title: PYZ: A Hold Despite Its Concentrated Portfolio Of Inflation Beneficiaries Prone To Deep Drawdowns
data.description:
I am of the opinion that in the current environment, the Invesco Dorsey Wright Basic Materials Momentum ETF (PYZ) does not offer a risk/reward profile appealing enough despite delivering quite an impressive performance YTD.

While the oil price-related inflation narrative is slowly developing amid the U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict, and PYZ might look like an ideal vehicle to play it, as it offers exposure to the raciest stocks from the industry that is deemed one of the key beneficiaries of high inflation, its historical performance warrants caution.

In fact, PYZ has underperformed IVV since the index change in February 2014, delivering a 1.6x higher standard deviation, as well as deeper maximum drawdowns during the pandemic, the 2022 bear market, and the 2025 tariff drama. Besides, compared to the State Street Materials Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLB), PYZ's strategy does not add much to annualized returns but significantly boosts volatility. As a risk-adjusted returns-focused investor, I find this quite worrisome and disappointing.

What also feeds my skepticism are its comparatively small AUM of $101.4 million, a fairly wide median bid/ask spread of 20 bps, and high net expense ratio of 60 bps. Overall, owing to its mix of benefits and disadvantages, I believe a Hold rating is the optimal one to initiate coverage with.

PYZ Strategy And Portfolio

As we know from the Invesco website, the keystone of PYZ's strategy is the Dorsey Wright® Basic Materials Technical Leaders Index. As explained in the prospectus:

Strictly in accordance with its guidelines and mandated procedures,Dorsey, Wright & Associates, LLC (“Dorsey Wright” or the “Index Provider”) compiles and maintains the Underlying Index, which is composed of at least 30 securities of companies in the basic materials sector that have powerful relative strength or “momentum” characteristics. Dorsey Wright selects these securities from 2,000 of the largest constituents by market capitalization within the Nasdaq US Benchmark™ Index, a float adjusted market capitalization-weighted index designed to track the performance of the U.S. equity market. “Relative strength” is an investing technique that seeks to determine the strongest performing securities by measuring certain factors, such as a security’s relative performance against the overall market over a set period or a security’s relative strength value, which is derived by comparing the rate of increase of the security’s price as compared to that of a benchmark index.

Importantly, while PYZ has quite a long history of more than 19 years, we have to ignore a significant portion of it, as its underlying index was changed in 2014. More specifically, as explained in the fact sheet, before February 19, 2014, PYZ's underlying index was the Dynamic Basic Materials Sector Intellidex Index.

PYZ Portfolio: Mostly Metals & Mining And Chemical Players With Strong Growth Characteristics

As of March 2, there were 46 equities in the PYZ portfolio, with the key ten accounting for 41.8% of the net assets. The top allocations were metals & mining (45.5% weight) and chemicals (33.2% weight), as per the Invesco website (as of February 28).

PYZ's largest position was Carpenter Technology Corporation (CRS), with a 5.1% weight. CRS is an aerospace & defense industry (the industrials sector) name generating the bulk of its net sales from specialty alloys. CRS' YTD price return is 30.9% as of writing this article before the market open on March 5, so its presence in the momentum stock portfolio is hardly surprising.

However, it might look quite confusing how an industrial company managed to qualify for a portfolio of basic materials players, and it is my pleasure to provide a clarification. As explained in the methodology available on the index's website, to qualify for inclusion, a security must be "designated to a sector based on DWA®’s proprietary industry classification system." In other words, there is a chance that companies outside the GICS materials sector will qualify. And in the current iteration of the PYZ portfolio, in addition to the above-mentioned CRS, there is also one GICS energy sector player, Uranium Energy Corp. (UEC), and another industrial name, ATI Inc. (ATI). And my calculations show its exposure to the GICS materials sector at 86.1%.

PYZ vs. XLB and IVV: Factor Mix

How is the PYZ portfolio different from that of XLB? Given the profound differences in their methodologies, it is hardly surprising that exposures they are offering are anything but identical.

PYZ does have some overlap with the XLB portfolio but only a moderate one. More specifically, my calculations show that eleven S&P 500 constituents from the GICS materials sector, key among them being Newmont Corporation (NEM), qualified for PYZ as well, now having 29.74% weight. In XLB, they account for around 54.5% of the net assets.

So, what we see under the hood is, compared to both XLB and IVV, which I added to ensure a broader context, a quality-light, high-growth, and high-beta mix. In fairness, I am not a fan of this combination (especially of quality characteristics), yet it has nonetheless worked for PYZ this year so far.

Speaking about growth, a nuance here is that I adjusted the weighted average forward revenue growth rate by downsizing UEC's contribution: the stock has a 3.97% weight in the PYZ portfolio and a 795.39% forward revenue growth rate, which I reduced to 100% to improve the reliability of the final result. Most names in the PYZ basket do not offer revenue growth rates that impressive: only 39.7% have that figure at over 10% (which is still higher than XLB's 22.5% allocation to such names).

PYZ Performance: High Volatility, Soft Risk-Adjusted Returns

PYZ's performance in 2026 has been outstanding with no doubt, and so was its return last year, when it beat IVV by around 10.15%.

Moreover, it has also managed to outmaneuver the Vanguard Materials Index Fund ETF (VAW) and XLB, even though the iShares U.S. Basic Materials ETF (IYM) and the First Trust Materials AlphaDEX Fund (FXZ) have been racier.

For context, in the PYZ portfolio, 27 names (74.5% weight) have delivered a YTD price return of over 10% as of March 4. In XLB, similarly successful stocks accounted for 73.7% (19 names).

However, I would like to zoom out and focus on the bigger picture, since there is a lot to dislike when data tracing back to 2014 is factored in, especially on the risk front.

So, since the index change on February 19, 2014, PYZ has outperformed VAW and FXZ but by just a few bps. And this minor outperformance is primarily driven by its YTD result, while the period from 2020 to approximately early 2025 looked quite soft. Most importantly, it underperformed XLB while keeping pace with IVV was way beyond its capabilities.

Next, over March 2014–February 2026, PYZ delivered a weaker annualized return compared to XLB, VAW, and IVV.

  • But a much bigger problem here is its risk-adjusted returns (the Sharpe and Sortino ratios) that were the worst in the group, owing to its high volatility.
  • And the maximum drawdown of -41.16% delivered during the pandemic was also the worst in the group. For context, during the 2022 bear market, its maximum drawdown was -28.77% vs. XLB's -23.73%.
  • And in April 2025, during the tariff drama, it was again much more vulnerable, with a maximum drawdown of -15.85% vs. XLB's -12.09%.
  • Besides, PYZ captured over 141% of IVV's downside (and over 117% of XLB's), which is, of course, quite a disappointing result.

Final Thoughts

In sum, the U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict creates at least tactical opportunities in basic materials as supply chain disruptions, with the Strait of Hormuz situation at the center of them, look supportive of comparatively higher inflation, though the implications are anything but certain at this point. However, PYZ is a highly volatile vehicle, with not particularly impressive historical returns and quite deep maximum drawdowns. In this regard, I am not advocating for a bullish view while also not joining the bearish camp, as betting against momentum stocks in the basic materials universe (and especially in petrochemicals) in the current environment can go very wrong quickly.

data.title: The New Defensive Playbook: Defense Spending As A Long-Term Reality
data.description:
By Samuel Rines and Jonathan Flynn

From Headline Risk to Structural Reality

Geopolitical risk has moved from a background concern to a central portfolio variable. Institutional surveys increasingly rank it as one of the most difficult risks to model, surpassing even inflation in some cases.

Yet today’s environment is not simply about short-term conflict headlines. It reflects a deeper transformation: a sustained global rearmament cycle, driven by modernization, technological competition and shifting alliances.

For investors, the key question is not whether tensions will fluctuate. It is whether defense spending, particularly in Europe and Asia, has entered a durable growth phase. We believe it has.

A Multi-Decade Rearmament Cycle Is Underway

Recent events have highlighted the effectiveness of modern Western defense systems, particularly interceptors, radar, drones and electronic detection. At the same time, they have exposed the rapid depletion of existing inventories.

That combination matters.

Defense contractors are not just working through existing backlogs. They are moving into what could be described as “far logs”, orders extending well into the next decade. Replenishment alone can stretch delivery timelines from three to five years out to 2030 and beyond.

This dynamic is not dependent on perpetual conflict. Even in a calmer geopolitical environment, countries are rebuilding inventories, upgrading systems and hardening domestic defense capabilities. In many cases, the modernization push is structural and bipartisan.

Defense is increasingly resembling a subscription model for governments, steady, recurring and essential.

The Pivot to Modern Warfare

One of the most important shifts investors may be underappreciating is the nature of modern defense.

This is no longer just about aircraft carriers or traditional heavy hardware. Increasingly, it is about:

  • Drone systems and counter-drone technology
  • Radar and early detection systems
  • Small-munition interception
  • AI-driven battlefield analytics
  • Advanced communications and electronic components

In Europe, where large-cap technology ecosystems are limited, defense companies are often at the forefront of advanced engineering and R&D. In Asia, particularly Japan and South Korea, electronic components and precision interception technologies are global leaders.

Defense innovation has historically spilled into civilian technology. The Cold War catalyzed many of the foundational technologies that underpin today’s digital economy. It would be premature to assume the current cycle will not generate similar second-order innovations.

For investors, this means the “industrial” label often attached to these companies understates their technological depth.

Valuations and the “Overbought” Question

After strong performance, investors naturally ask: Has the opportunity already played out?

It is important to distinguish between price momentum and structural earnings visibility.

Defense firms in Europe and Asia are benefiting from:

  • Expanding order books
  • Government commitments to multi-year spending increases
  • Inventory replenishment cycles
  • Technological upgrading

These factors provide forward revenue clarity that is uncommon in many equity sectors.

Even if geopolitical tensions ease in the near term, spending trajectories are unlikely to reverse. Defense budgets, once increased, rarely retreat meaningfully, especially amid rising global fragmentation and great-power competition.

Why Broad International Exposure May Miss the Theme

Many investors assume they already own this opportunity through broad international or emerging markets ETFs. In reality, that exposure is minimal.

Traditional international indexes are often dominated by financials and value-oriented sectors. Defense allocations are typically small, and overlap with dedicated defense strategies is limited.

This creates two important portfolio benefits:

  1. Low overlap and differentiation from traditional international holdings.
  2. Lower correlation relative to U.S. growth-heavy benchmarks.

For investors seeking diversification away from concentrated U.S. technology exposure, European and Asian defense equities represent a distinct return stream.

Portfolio Application: Targeted Access to Europe, Asia and Global Defense

WisdomTree provides targeted exposure through:

  • The WisdomTree Europe Defense Fund (WDEF)
  • The WisdomTree Asia Defense Fund (WDAF)
  • The WisdomTree Global Defense Fund (WDGF)

These strategies offer access to companies that are often difficult to purchase directly on U.S. exchanges and may lack liquid American Depositary Receipts.

These funds focus on firms positioned to benefit from modernization, technological advancement and expanding global defense budgets. Sector exposure skews toward industrials, but with meaningful embedded technology components.

For investors looking to diversify U.S. equity exposure, particularly concentrated positions in mega-cap growth, these strategies can serve as complementary allocations with differentiated drivers.

Conclusion: A Structural Shift, not a Tactical Trade

Europe and key Asian allies are building independent and technologically advanced defense capabilities. This buildout appears durable, bipartisan and multi-year.

For investors, the takeaway is clear: defense spending in Europe, Asia and globally has moved beyond a headline-driven trade. It is a structural allocation story, supported by modernization, replenishment and innovation.

In a world where geopolitical risk is harder to model than inflation, targeted exposure to global defense may serve not only as an opportunity, but as a portfolio stabilizer.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including potential loss of principal. Foreign investing involves specific risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit, and the Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index. The composition of the Index is governed by an Index Committee and the Index may not perform as intended. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

WDEF: A Fund focusing its investments on certain sectors and/or regions increases its vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development. This may result in greater share price volatility.

WDAF: Funds focusing their investments on certain sectors increase their vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development. This may result in greater share price volatility.

Because the Fund invests primarily in the securities of companies in Asia, the Fund’s performance is expected to be closely tied to social, political, and economic conditions within Asia and to be more volatile than the performance of more geographically diversified funds. Many countries in the region have historically faced political uncertainty, corruption, military intervention, and social unrest. To the extent that such events continue in the future, they can be expected to have an unpredictable effect on economic and securities market conditions in the region and may impact the ability of the Fund to buy, sell or otherwise transfer securities and cause the Fund to decline in value.

Investments in non-U.S. securities involve political, regulatory, and economic risks that may not be present in investments in U.S. securities.

WDGF: To the extent the Fund invests a significant portion of its assets in securities of companies of a single country or region, it is more likely to be impacted by events or conditions affecting that country or region. Investments in non-U.S. securities involve political, regulatory, and economic risks that may not be present in investments in U.S. securities.

Investments in non-U.S. securities may be subject to risk of loss due to foreign currency fluctuations, political or economic instability, or geographic events that adversely impact issuers of foreign securities. Investments in non-U.S. securities also may be subject to withholding or other taxes and may be subject to additional trading, settlement, custodial, and operational risks. These and other factors can make investments in the Fund more volatile and potentially less liquid than other types of investments. These risks may be heightened to the extent the Fund invests in companies domiciled in or otherwise tied to developing or emerging market countries.

Samuel Rines, Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios

Sam Rines serves as a Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios at WisdomTree extending the firm’s custom model portfolio management capabilities. Prior to WisdomTree, Samuel was a Managing Director of CORBU, a research firm we struck up a relationship with to deliver model portfolios. Samuel is a global macro expert focused on the investment implications of politics and policy. His PolyMacro research has been widely followed by large family offices, institutional investors and the media. Prior to joining CORBU, Samuel was the Chief Economist and Investment Strategist at Avalon Advisors. Before joining Avalon, Samuel was a portfolio manager at Chilton Capital Management, where he launched the Chilton ESG Equity Strategy and a long/short technology portfolio. Samuel started his career as the cross-asset analyst for a small hedge fund. He is the author of the book “After Normal: Making Sense of the Global Economy”.

Jonathan Flynn, Director of Market Strategy

data.title: Why February's CPI Report Matters More Than Ever Amid War-Driven Inflation
data.description:
A month after the Bureau of Labor Statistics favorably low January Consumer Price Index figures, it will release February’s report on Wednesday, March 11. The upcoming report matters more than ever for two main reasons. First, energy inflation risks are in play in March, so investors would need to wait another month before the official CPI figures. Secondly, the January CPI of 0.2% is 30 bps lower than the Producer Price Index of 0.5%.

What should investors and consumers expect from the February CPI report?

January CPI Increased 0.2%

The BLS reported a CPI 0.2% increase. As usual, the index for shelter (+0.2%) accounted for most of the all items monthly increase. The index for energy (-1.5%) helped offset the headline figure. From a topline view, services topped the increase that month. The index for food (+0.2%) is still above the Fed’s annual 2.0% inflation target rate (2.9%).

Stock markets continued to price in weaker demand for ride-hailing in the last few months. Shares in both Uber and Lyft fell in 2026. Flying-vehicle firm Joby Aviation (JOBY), which is one of Uber’s Seeking Alpha Peers, fell by over 25% YTD.

Uber is ranked first in the industry and 232nd in the industrial sector. This is an unfair ranking for the firm, since aerospace and defense is 24.2% of the market weight in this measure.

Amid the ongoing war economy, RTX (RTX), Boeing (BA), and General Electric (GE) are among the top industrial stocks. Uber is in eighth place with a 1.99% market weight.

Index for Medicare Care Services

The index for medical care services increased by 3.9% in the 12-months in the January report. Investors should expect this to increase again in February. The Federal Reserve Bank of New York recognized that higher health insurance costs dampened wage growth. GLP-1 obesity drugs skew the higher healthcare spending, helping Lilly (LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO). Still, Fed Chair Jerome Powell counted on Americans benefiting from wage growth to counter the impact inflation has on eroding the consumer’s disposable income.

In the drug manufacturing sector, readers may also consider Pfizer (PFE), AbbVie (ABBV), and Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY):

In the Quant scores above, Pfizer and Novo both score an A- or higher on valuation. Lilly has the highest industry rank of 20/177.

Warning on Energy Services Inflation

The index for energy services might increase again in February. It rose by 0.2% in January and by 7.2% in the year. To prevent consumers from blaming AI data centers, the government pressured tech companies to pay for AI data center power costs.

Companies in the energy services sector to consider include SLB (SLB), Baker Hughes (BKR), and Halliburton (HAL). Shares are up by at least 20% in 2026:

Investors would pay a premium, since the valuation grade is a D- or lower. Readers might want to wait for the momentum grade to weaken.

In the infrastructure and services sector, consider Williams Companies (WMB), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), and Kinder Morgan (KMI).

Above: EPD stock has the best valuation grade of “B.” However, it scores the worst on EPS revisions and on growth. All three firms have attractive profitability grades.

February Price Decrease

Expect the CPI for used cars and trucks to decline like it did in January. Contrast that trend with new vehicle prices that increased. After the Iran invasion, shares of Ford Motor (F) and General Motors (GM) fell meaningfully. F stock lost 14% in the last week, while GM stock fell by around 6%.
