The military conflict in Iran might lead to an increase in gas prices by 15% to 20%. Oil shipments through the Strait of Hormuz hurt global oil supplies. Additionally, the gas price increase includes the annual switch from winter to summer gas. Summer gas costs more to produce, so consumers should brace for an uptrend in the price of gasoline. That would hurt the demand for new and used vehicles.

Note On January’s Producer Price Index

The BLS reported the Producer Price Index for final demand rose by 0.5%. The index for final demand services increased 0.8%. The sharp rise is a result of margins for final demand trade services (+2.5%). Look out for the impact of inflation on wholesalers and retailers showing up for consumers in future CPI reports.

Your Takeaway

Stock markets are forward-looking. So, traders should not expect markets to react much to the upcoming CPI report. Instead, it will monitor the continued tensions in Iran. Moreover, shipment delays through the Strait of Hormuz will sway markets in the near-term.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Vermilion Energy: A Deep Value Natural Gas Play That's Still Too Cheap
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Vermilion Energy (VET), I reiterated their Strong Buy rating, highlighting their significant discount to both intrinsic and book value, with solid assets and an improving balance sheet, while the company's US exit and portfolio refinancing supported a potential re-rating.

Despite the stock now being up about 30% since the previous coverage in January and over 50% since the initial rating back in late October, I reiterate VET’s Strong Buy rating, as the company continues to improve and remains on a good trajectory, while the valuation continues to be very attractive.

Internal Developments

VET reported a good Q4 and 2025 overall, beating the market's revenue estimates, delivering record annual production, and exceeding their Q4 production guidance, with very solid improvements in unit costs and CAPEX/flowing boe estimated for 2026 thanks to their portfolio recycling.

This is ultimately something very important that I’m trying to highlight about this company. Sure, nobody is denying that they’ve had bad management in the past, they’ve made mistakes, allocated capital inefficiently, etc., but many of these managers/directors are gone, and we could even call this a massive, top-to-bottom fundamental transformation, changing significantly in recent years, growing reserves per share, cutting costs, and building a strong portfolio with long-life assets.

Despite the high CAPEX and macro pressure, we can see their strategy paying out already, with the free cash flow reaching a solid C$308.74 million (US$226.24 million) in 2025 compared to C$344.77 million in 2024, which is not bad at all for a ~US$1.75 billion market cap after their jump.

VET also confirmed their full-year guidance, expecting C$600 million to C$630 million in CAPEX (similar to 2025), with 67% of that going into Canada and 33% on their international assets, while the production will continue to be heavily focused on natural gas, further supporting this global transition/portfolio repositioning.

Financially, based on VET’s latest report, we can see a solid position, even though the current assets stand below the current liabilities and their cash and cash equivalents dropped, while they reduced the net debt by over C$700 million (targeting more reductions in the future as they aim to reach a net debt to FFO leverage of 1.0x) since the jump earlier last year as a result of the Westbrick acquisition from about a year ago that expanded their Deep Basin exposure, and I’d add that the repayment and balance sheet focus are a good move in this environment.

VET also increased the dividend by 4% recently to US$0.395 per year (C$0.135 per quarter), for a roughly 3.6% yield at the moment of writing this, as well as C$35.75 million (US$26.16 million) in buybacks, for an additional ~1.5% yield. Combined with ~US$60 million in dividends and based on the kind of cash flow VET is able to produce, we're talking about a very sustainable payout ratio, and there is plenty of cash flow left to improve the balance sheet or focus on additional growth or returns.

Macro Developments

To begin with the attack against Iran, we can see a different initial response than expected, followed by Iran’s threat regarding the Strait of Hormuz, which is key for international oil, gas, fertilizers, etc., and the subsequent strikes launched by the country against its neighbors.

Although Trump said the US Navy could escort ships through the strait, this conflict is very important on a global scale and can go well beyond it, as it involves countries that are behind the Petrodollar’s strength and natural gas/LNG giants such as Qatar - which produces roughly 20% of global LNG - with the latter’s recent output halt following Iran’s strikes leading to a ~50% jump in European gas on the first day of trading following it, while fuel prices also rose.

To briefly mention Venezuela as well, I’ll reiterate that I believe Exxon (XOM) summarized it perfectly as “uninvestible,” as even though we’re talking about the biggest reserves on the planet, we’re also looking at one of the heaviest oils (needs a lot of processing) and ancient infrastructure, all on top of political and economic pressure that makes taking this risk extremely hard to justify to shareholders, especially at a time when many players are cutting their CAPEX.

Still, Exxon said a few days ago that they’re going to send a team to Venezuela in a few weeks, although it could still take many years until they're back in the country (they left in 2007).

There are several differences to note here, however, and if the final goal is a regime change (and maintaining it), this situation may last for way more than Trump’s expected few weeks. Again, we’re talking about a natural fortress that has been preparing for something like this for decades, which lacks the kind of organized/armed opposition that the previous (failed) attempts that involved American soldiers on the ground (e.g. Afghanistan, Vietnam - roughly 2 decades each), on top of being more than twice the size of Afghanistan, having four-season weather, mountains, all of which are very important differences from a military point of view, and I don’t think anyone is ready for that.

Sure, this could be just a short-term “push” for negotiations, but every bit of uncertainty and high fuel prices adds more pressure on an already fragile economy, marked by significant consumer weakness as a result of the rate-hike slowdown accentuated by tariffs and other macro uncertainty.

OPEC+ has so far been acting as expected, recently approving a modest boost for April a day after the attack, but they could be a major winner in case commodity prices continue their jump, although potential for higher production hikes could act as a ceiling to how high they can go, as the demand side can also be affected.

Looking at it from a fundamental point of view, we’re still talking about a structural surplus, and if there's no long-term blockade or conflict in the region, there’s a high chance that the fundamental pressures have the final word on the price, as production from many countries is at record highs (especially if high prices persist) despite weak demand (which would also be affected by high prices), even though there’s a chance that near-term prices could even reach $100/bbl, as said very well by Wood Mackenzie analysts recently:

In this conflict, the stakes are higher still with 15 million b/d of Gulf crude and product exports under threat. Failure to quickly re-establish flows through the Strait of Hormuz could again drive prices well over US$100/bbl from Friday’s close under US$73/bbl (Brent).

As for the longer term, LNG’s potential remains very strong thanks to its role as a transition fuel and AI’s massive energy demand that can support significant demand, with studies highlighting potential for very high CAGRs (11.6% from 2025 to 2030) and significant shifts following the war in Ukraine.

Meanwhile, since VET has strong natural gas exposure and doesn’t depend solely on local AECO prices, they can continue to enjoy international prices, especially as US and even Canadian LNG projects come online, potentially lifting local prices as well since the premium wouldn’t have to be so deep.

At the same time, we can see a shift in Canada’s stance on oil and gas following the Grand Bargain MOU, with the country likely aiming at cutting its dependency on the US, and I’ll also mention that the country has significant potential as a top data center location given their cheap resources, weather and potential for regulatory support, with Alberta launching a dedicated “AI Data Centre Strategy” to attract $100 billion in investments about a year ago, while the federal government is also reportedly working on something in this direction, with some proposals announced a few months ago.

Risks Reiterated

Overall, although I remain very bullish on Canadian oil and especially gas and can definitely see a permanent re-rating/re-pricing in their assets going forward, VET will always play in a very volatile market, with significant impacts from politics, wars, economics, speculation, etc., ultimately impacting their valuation and performance.

This is very important to keep in mind, as I see more and more concerns about various oil and gas producers whose stocks are underperforming, even though it’s ultimately normal since they remain tied to the commodity.

Of course, management and past mistakes also play a role in this underperformance, and even though I see more upside for VET, we should keep in mind that their focus on Canada is not guaranteed to deliver. And, even a couple of weeks ago, we’ve just seen Australian regulators shutting down their legacy Wandoo’s exports after an oil spill in December and repeated “systemic failures” in their environmental management systems, so we really shouldn’t ignore this kind of risk.

Valuation

For VET's DCF to Equity valuation, assuming constant commodity prices going forward, I'll go for the same $200 million FCF I expect in 2026 (note that everything in this valuation is converted in USD), thanks to it being a transition year, followed by a jump to $300 million in 2027 thanks to their CAPEX falling, German project entering first production and ramping up soon, growing at a 3.5% CAGR until 2030 thanks to other improvements, interest reductions, capital efficiencies, etc., followed by a 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate given the potential of Canada and natural gas, with a 12.5% discount rate to take into account an even better margin of safety given the volatility, as well as 155.18 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

Note that I won’t account for net borrowings here, as I want to highlight their pure cash generation potential, and I believe the current level of debt is manageable, especially since their leverage can be reduced through FFO growth instead.

This way, we get to an equity value of $2.95 billion, for an intrinsic value of $19.00, which is still well above the current $11.00 in the post-market at the moment of writing this.

Conclusions

I reiterate VET’s Strong Buy rating, even though the stock is up significantly since I started covering them, backed by a strong and improving portfolio, solid financials, and very good cash flows.

There are risks to keep in mind, as we’re talking about a commodity company first and foremost, but I remain very bullish on natural gas and Canada overall, and VET remains very undervalued from many points of view.

data.title: SPY Is Now A Tech ETF: Building A Quantitatively Optimized 'Strong Buy' Alternative
data.description:
Introduction

The S&P 500 is currently historically concentrated in the largest companies. In the last ten years, that trend has been a huge tailwind for passive ETF investors, as more of their capital was unwittingly allocated to mega-cap stocks experiencing huge growth. However, with the S&P so heavily concentrated in the top few stocks by market cap, the pendulum may be ready to swing back in the other direction, favoring custom portfolio construction once again.

Seeking Alpha’s “Strong Buy” picks provide investors on the platform a tool to identify high-quality stocks. However, in investing, picking the best stocks is only half the battle. In this article, I aim to leverage Seeking Alpha’s quantitative ratings to create a portfolio consisting of great stock picks that is also quantitatively optimized. I use techniques of Markowitz mean-variance optimization, Monte Carlo simulation, and Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage to create an optimal portfolio of “Strong Buy” picks as of March 1st, 2026.

Stocks as Behavioral Entities

The key insight of modern portfolio theory, as I see it, is that in addition to a stock’s underlying fundamentals, it also has behavioral characteristics. That means that a portfolio can be more than the sum of its parts if it consists of stocks that have complementary behaviors. Similar to how an organization with a myriad of different personality types might function better than one in which every person is a similar type of personality, a portfolio that consists of stocks that have different “personalities” is likely to perform better on a risk-adjusted basis. As Harry Markowitz famously said, “Diversification is the only free lunch in investing.”

Most investors assume that buying an S&P 500 index fund is the best way to achieve diversification. But in 2026, that assumption may be losing its validity. Currently, the top 10 names in the S&P 500 dominate the index, with 41% of the combined market capitalization (a record high). And those top 10 names are typically stocks involved in AI and tech. Out of the top 10, only Berkshire Hathaway is not a tech company. These top ten companies tend to be highly correlated with one another, which is the opposite of what you would want in a diversified portfolio. The chart below shows the degree to which the top 10 has dominated the S&P index over the last 65 years:

So investors buying index funds that track the major indexes like the S&P 500 and Nasdaq may not realize that they’re exposed to significant concentration risk by investing in an index fund. So far, S&P ETFs have done well because the top companies have done so well, but that is not guaranteed to be the case going forward. For someone buying SPY or IVV in 2014, they would have been getting a more diversified portfolio than someone buying those ETFs today is. There are also “equal weight” S&P 500 ETFs, but equal weighting still doesn’t achieve optimal diversification because you might have a relatively high number of similar companies in the index.

So that got me thinking: what if we combine Seeking Alpha’s stock ranking system with quantitative portfolio optimization to select a portfolio that both consists of stocks that have strong fundamentals and that achieves the “free lunch” of optimal diversification? I’m a strong believer in both Buffett-style fundamental analysis and portfolio optimization, so in this project I aim to combine the two to the greatest effect I can.

Methodology

Stock Selection

I used Seeking Alpha’s screening tool to narrow down the universe of stocks for inclusion in this portfolio. I selected US-based stocks with Quant “Strong Buy” ratings with market caps over $4 billion and individual share prices under $5000. That narrowed the list down to 89 stocks. I then pulled adjusted closing prices for these stocks into Python using the yfinance API. I narrowed the data down to prices from January 1, 2017, to today and eliminated 11 stocks from consideration that had missing data (likely companies that were not publicly traded for the entire period). That left 78 stocks to use as a basis for constructing this optimized portfolio. You can download the CSV file I used for this project here: Strong_Buys_List.csv

Modeling Expected Returns and Volatility

One way to model future expected returns and volatilities is to simply use observed average return and observed volatility (historical standard deviation). This approach tends to work well for volatility but less well for actual return. So I used a few statistical techniques to attempt to model expected returns more accurately. First, I winsorized the distribution of daily returns for each stock, which means that I trimmed the extreme outliers from the data (the top 2.5% of daily returns take the value of the 97.5th percentile, and the bottom 2.5% of returns take the value of the 2.5th percentile). That way, huge gains or losses like +/- 30% in one day don’t affect the expected return value. Then, rather than just calculating the average daily return and multiplying by 252 to get an annual return, I simulated a year of trading by randomly selecting 252 daily returns from the winsorized data of daily returns from 2017 to present for each stock. I repeated the simulation 1000 times for each stock and used the median result for each stock as a proxy for its expected return and volatility.

By simulating expected return and expected volatility, we can determine the top and bottom picks based on their “synthetic” Sharpe ratios (using a risk-free rate of 4%). The chart below shows the top 5 and bottom 5 stocks based on simulated Sharpe ratios:

Note the best stocks by synthetic Sharpe ratio may not be the top holdings in the optimized portfolio. For example, Arista Networks (ANET) and Lumentum Holdings (LITE) both have very high Sharpe ratios, but the two stocks are also highly correlated with each other (i.e., they exhibit similar behavioral characteristics). So having a heavy concentration of both stocks in a portfolio might be like trying to play with two quarterbacks. Patrick Mahomes and Lamar Jackson may both be great players, but you wouldn’t want both on the same team.

Calculating the Optimal Portfolio

The first step in optimizing the portfolio is constructing a covariance matrix. I used the Ledoit-Wolf estimator to create the covariance matrix used in the optimization calculation. Studies have found that this method tends to produce better estimates of true covariance than a standard covariance matrix does.

Next, I run a sequential least squares programming algorithm to determine the optimal portfolio weights, with the following constraints:

  • No single holding can make up more than 12% of the overall portfolio, and no single holding can be less than 0% of the portfolio (no short-selling).

  • The sum of all weights must equal 1

All 78 stocks were given some weight in the optimized portfolio, but some weights were so low as to be impractical to actually include. So I narrowed down the portfolio to only the stocks that had a greater than 1% weight in the optimized portfolio, and I ran the optimization algorithm again to get final weightings. This produced a portfolio of 15 stocks. The following graphic shows the weights in the final optimized portfolio and how that portfolio compares in risk-adjusted terms to an equal-weighted portfolio of all 78 Strong Buy stocks:

Interestingly, we get lower volatility from a smaller portfolio compared to a portfolio of 78 equal-weighted stocks. Typically, historical volatility is a good proxy for future volatility, so although I am taking some liberties in forecasting expected returns, the expected volatility of the portfolio is likely to be a decent estimate of actual volatility. I also calculated the average return and volatility of SPY over the same time frame: the annualized return is 14.78%, and the annualized volatility is 18.38%. So SPY, with 500 holdings, seems to be more volatile than the optimized portfolio of 15 holdings, while the optimized portfolio is expected to achieve significantly higher returns (albeit based on historical simulation assumptions). This shows how combining even relatively few complementary assets in the right proportions can perform better in both risk and return than simply owning everything.

The top five holdings show how the algorithm sought diversified selection. IESC (Industrials - Construction/Engineering), LMT (Industrials - Defense & Aerospace), PM (Consumer Staples), AU (Materials - Gold), and ANET (Information Technology) span very diverse sectors/industries. The correlation matrix of the top 8 holdings helps explain why these selections go well together:

AU is a particularly strong pick because of its low correlation with all the other top 8 stocks. ANET, LITE, and IESC have quite high correlation between them, but that intercorrelation is balanced out by low correlations with stocks like LMT, PM, and AU.

Interestingly, the quantitative optimization seemed to gravitate toward what I would call a “barbell approach.” This table shows the stocks in the optimized portfolio, their sector, industry, and approximate beta. You can see that we have a bifurcation between several higher-beta IT stocks (or stocks orbiting the AI/data center cycle) and then lower-beta stocks in Industrials/Materials/Consumer Staples/Healthcare:

The overall beta for the portfolio is 0.96, indicating a similar level of risk to the overall S&P 500.

Backtesting the Methodology

To verify that the methodology actually works, we can pretend we’re at the start of a prior period of time (say 2024) and see what the model would have recommended at that time and then how it would have performed. So we make the model blind to data after January 2024 and see how well it would have worked.

Running the algorithm on pre-2024 data produced a portfolio of 14 stocks. Some of the picks are overlapping - ANET, AU, BOOT, IESC, LMT, DDS, MU - while others are new - AMD, AMZN, BRK.B, DELL, MOD, RUSHA, STLD. The predicted return for the portfolio in 2024 was 27%, with 22% volatility, while actual results were 48.6% return with 24.8% volatility. So both in absolute terms and in risk-adjusted terms, the selected portfolio outperformed the expected metrics.

The major limitation to this backtest is that I don’t have access to Seeking Alpha’s “Strong Buys” at that time, so we’re using today’s “Strong Buy” list as a stand-in. The “Strong Buy” rated stocks would have been different at the start of 2024 than they are now. So that does limit the ability to truly backtest the methodology, since it introduces the possibility of survivorship bias and look-ahead bias. Just looking at some of these tickers, some were on the “Strong Buy” list in January 2024, like ANET and AU, while quite a few were rated “Hold” - AMZN, LMT, and MU for example.

Limitations & Word of Warning

I want to make it very clear that quantitative portfolio optimization hinges heavily on return assumptions for the individual stock picks. To estimate returns for individual stocks, I try to use a more robust methodology than simply using the historical average return. However, I am still using historical data to predict the future, which is an assumption that does a lot of heavy lifting in the analysis.

So that this analysis doesn’t rely too heavily on return assumptions, I also calculated the weights for a minimum variance portfolio of the “Strong Buy” stocks (stripping out any assumptions about expected returns). Here are the weights for that portfolio:

So this portfolio would be optimal for an investor seeking the lowest volatility out of the universe of “Strong Buy” stocks. It is clearly more skewed toward the lower beta names, but there is still substantial overlap with the stocks picked in the risk-return optimized portfolio.

Another thing to consider is that optimizing a portfolio in this way is a bit of a moving target. Ever since I started this analysis, one stock (IESC) has dropped from a Strong Buy to a Hold rating. This seems to be due to a change in the EPS Rev score from A+ to F. As new stocks become Strong Buys and current Strong Buys lose their status, the portfolio optimization will change. So the specific constitution of the portfolio will depend on when we gather data and run the algorithm.

Final Thoughts

What should the takeaway be for investors? This project is meant to be a demonstrative exercise. You don’t necessarily need to imitate this exact portfolio, but I believe the lesson to be learned here is that it may be a good time to construct diversified portfolios of individual stocks (along with other assets potentially) again. For quite a long time the prevailing belief has been that passive investing in ETFs is a superior strategy to active stock picking and portfolio construction. That may have been true when indexes like the S&P 500 were well diversified, but when a few mega-cap stocks make up such a large percentage of the index, then an ETF tracking that index no longer represents optimal diversification in my view. Furthermore, even at other points in history during which the S&P 500 was dominated by the top 10 stocks (1960, 2000), those stocks were not all thematically concentrated. In 2000, the top 10 consisted of stocks like Microsoft, GE, Walmart, Exxon Mobil, and Pfizer. Even though there was heavy concentration in the top 10 at that time, they still spanned different sectors and industries.

So even though passive ETF investing has worked well in recent history, it gathers more risk as more market cap is sucked up into the top IT stocks. Therefore, I assign a “Hold” rating to the various S&P 500 ETFs (SPY, IVV, VOO). In my view, owning these ETFs alone is no longer an optimal investment strategy. I believe that investors can achieve better risk-adjusted returns by owning a diverse basket of stocks or ETFs instead of just owning a single index fund.

If you enjoyed this analysis and would like to see it updated/improved upon later this year, let me know in the comments. If people are interested, I can add a link to view and download the Jupyter Notebook file for this project in the comments as well. Thanks for reading!

data.title: Insteel Industries Is Getting Close To An Upgrade
data.description:
Back in September of last year, I decided to dig back into Insteel Industries (IIIN), an interesting player in the building materials space. For those not familiar with the business, it produces prestressed concrete strand as well as welded wire reinforcements such as ESM. These are used heavily in the construction sector. And considering how large that is in the US, this is essentially a play on the American economy long term. As interesting as the company is, however, I have not been able to pull the trigger on an upgrade for the stock. For quite a while now, I have had it rated a "Hold." And at the time that article was published, I felt that upgrading it was premature.

Since then, the stock has risen 2.2%, falling just short of the 3.7% rise that the S&P 500 has seen. Since I originally downgraded it to a "Hold" in May 2024, the stock has risen 27.1%. This is only slightly worse than the 29.9% increase that the market enjoyed. Considering that a "Hold" candidate in my book is one that should perform along the lines of the broader market for the foreseeable future, I would say that my history with the company has been solid. And even though the stock has gotten cheaper in some respects, and even though we are seeing some rapid growth right now, I think that it would be a bit premature still to upgrade it.

I can’t steel my resolve to upgrade this play

Operationally speaking, I would say that Insteel Industries is an interesting prospect. The company has a relatively simple business model. And the end uses for what it sells can only be described as concentrated. About 70% of what the company generates in the way of revenue comes from concrete product manufacturers. And the remaining 30% involves distributors, rebar fabricators, and contractors. Overwhelmingly, the company is focused on the non-residential construction market. About 85% of its revenue comes from that space, with the remaining 15% coming from residential construction.

With this context provided, I would say that the overall financial performance for the business lately has not been shocking. As I have detailed in other articles, the residential construction space is under pressure right now. Years of inflationary pressures, followed by high interest rates aimed at combating them, not to mention other issues like tariffs, have proven harmful for the housing market. But thanks in large part to private industry making investments in things like data centers, as well as federal and state agencies continuing to allocate capital toward public infrastructure projects, the fact that 85% of the company's revenue comes from non-residential construction works well in its favor.

During the most recent quarter, which is the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year, revenue for the business came in at $159.9 million. This was an increase of 23.3% over the $129.7 million the business reported a year earlier. This sales increase was driven heavily by the welded wire reinforcement category of the firm. Overall revenue there jumped from $82.5 million to $108.4 million. That's a rise of 31.4% year over year. By comparison, prestressed concrete strand revenue rose a more modest 9.1% from $47.3 million to $51.6 million.

According to the business, the improvement that the company saw was driven in large part by higher average selling prices. In fact, that added 18.8% to the top line with the company. Management chalked this up to price increases aimed at recovering the escalation and raw material costs that the business was facing. In essence, when the firm has to contend with higher prices, it merely passes those on to consumers. Some of the price increase that the company saw was driven by tight domestic supply conditions. But a big contributor was clearly the Section 232 steel tariffs implemented by the current U.S. administration. To be clear, these tariffs on steel amount to 50%.

The fact of the matter is that the tariff situation both helps and hurts the company. This requires some context. Take wire rod as an example. This serves as the company's primary raw material. Total domestic production is about 3.5 million tons annually. Meanwhile, U.S. production of all hot-rolled carbon steel is about 100 million tons. But in recent years, the industry has seen the permanent closure of two producing mills and issues at a third one that have meaningfully reduced overall output. The result here is that actual domestic production of wire rod has fallen by more than 800,000 tons per year, while the domestic capacity to produce wire rod has fallen about 1.2 million tons per year compared to domestic consumption, which stands at about 5 million tons annually.

Management has calculated, essentially, that almost 25% of apparent domestic consumption is now offline. This has caused the company to look offshore for its raw materials. On the other hand, however, while the raw materials are costing the business more, the fact that it produces essentially all of its prestressed concrete strand, ESM, and concrete reinforcing products gives it a competitive advantage over firms that must produce and then import these products.

Despite this, the business actually did see some benefit from shipments rising as well. They grew 3.8% year over year. Management claimed that this was caused by favorable demand trends when it came to infrastructure and commercial construction markets, in addition to benefits associated with acquisition activities.

Management has been optimistic about non-residential construction thanks to robust infrastructure spending and data center activity. In the company's earnings call for the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, management talked about this in more detail. They actually said that they expect strength in the data center market to remain robust into next year. Outside of this, they said that other parts of the private non-residential construction market are weak right now. So they view this as a bridge until such time that other aspects of private industry rebound.

Thanks to higher revenue, profitability for the company improved also. Net income jumped from $1.1 million to $7.6 million. We saw operating cash flow plunge from $19 million to negative $0.7 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, however, we get a near doubling from $6.9 million to $12.5 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $6.1 million to $13.9 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2025 fiscal year compared to 2024. This showed continued growth on the top and bottom lines for the business. This is great to see, and to some extent there is evidence that the future for the firm might be even brighter. In the image below, you can see two leading indicators of the non-residential building construction space. The first of these is the Architectural Billings Index, or ABI for short. And the second is the Dodge Momentum Index, or DMI. Both of these indicators tell very different stories. The ABI, for instance, continues to be in a state of contraction.

Seeing as how it is a measure of architecture billings, it looks at demand that is usually out by 12 months or less. When these billings are contracting, it indicates that buildings are likely to be constructed at a slower pace than in the past. On its own, this is a negative for the company. But the DMI has shown something radically different. In recent months, this indicator, which tracks non-residential projects that are entering the planning stage, has skyrocketed. This means that there is a big jump in planning activity, and that usually materializes in the form of increased spending in the space more than a year out. This indicates that we are experiencing a short-term slowdown but that, beyond the next 12 months, strength is likely. This actually makes a lot of sense when you consider continued investments in data centers and the prospect of falling interest rates.

Unfortunately, while management has said that they are optimistic, we don't really have an idea what the rest of this year will look like. I think it would be more appropriate, then, to value the company in relation to financial performance for 2024 and 2025. Doing that, we can see in the chart below how the stock is priced. Honestly, using the 2025 figures, the stock isn't bad. I was even tempted to upgrade it, especially when you consider that it is trading on the cheap compared to other companies in the building materials space. In the chart below, for instance, you can see that on a price-to-earnings basis, only one of the five firms that I stacked it up against ended up being cheaper than it is. One of them was cheaper, and another was tied with it when it came to the price-to-operating cash flow approach. And finally, when it came to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, it ended up being the cheapest of the group.

Takeaway

I came closer to upgrading Insteel Industries today than I have the last couple of times I have written about it. I am especially enthusiastic about the DMI data. Having said that, I am concerned by broader economic conditions. My expectation is that the United States will likely enter into a recession before long. And while some aspects of spending will remain robust, others will weaken in the same way that the residential construction market has. So until we can see some changes on the economic front, or until we get some idea that the strength seen in the most recent quarter is part of a paradigm shift as opposed to a blip on the radar, I believe that maintaining the stock as a "Hold" is prudent.
